item.title: Architecture Decision Records
item.description:
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) are short text documents that capture a single architectural decision, its context, and its consequences. Michael Nygard introduced the format in a November 2011 blog post titled “Documenting Architecture Decisions,” published on the Cognitect blog (Nygard, 2011). The post argued that agile projects need lightweight documentation that stays close to the code, rather than large design documents that fall out of date.

Format

Nygard’s original ADR format draws on the structure of Alexandrian patterns. Each record contains five sections:

item.title: DevOps and Continuous Delivery as Planning Infrastructure
item.description: DevOps is a set of practices that unifies software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) to shorten the development lifecycle and deliver software changes more frequently. The term emerged around 2008–2009, with Patrick Debois organizing the first DevOpsDays conference in Ghent, Belgium, in 2009. The foundational texts are Jez Humble and David Farley’s Continuous Delivery (Addison-Wesley, 2010) and Gene Kim, Patrick Debois, John Willis, and Jez Humble’s The DevOps Handbook (IT Revolution Press, 2016).

item.title: Domain-Driven Design
item.description: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) is an approach to software development that organizes code and teams around a model of the business domain. Eric Evans introduced the approach in Domain-Driven Design: Tackling Complexity in the Heart of Software (Addison-Wesley, 2003). Evans argued that the primary complexity in most software projects is not technical but conceptual — it lies in the domain the software serves. DDD addresses this by making the domain model the central artifact around which development is organized.

item.title: RFC and Design Document Processes
item.description: Requests for Comments (RFCs) and design documents are written proposals that describe a planned technical change, solicit feedback from stakeholders, and build consensus before implementation begins. The practice originates with the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), where Steve Crocker wrote the first RFC in April 1969 to document the ARPANET’s host software protocols. The IETF process established the core pattern: write down the proposed change, invite comment, iterate, and reach agreement before committing to code.

item.title: Scrum Planning Structure
item.description: Scrum is a framework for developing and sustaining complex products, created by Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland. The two first presented Scrum at the OOPSLA conference in Austin, Texas, in 1995. Their approach drew on Hirotaka Takeuchi and Ikujiro Nonaka’s 1986 Harvard Business Review article “The New New Product Development Game,” which described cross-functional teams working in overlapping phases rather than sequential handoffs. The definitive specification is The Scrum Guide (Schwaber and Sutherland, 2020), now in its sixth revision.

item.title: Shape Up
item.description:
Shape Up is a product development method created by Ryan Singer at Basecamp (formerly 37signals). Singer compiled the method from Basecamp’s internal practices and published it in 2019 as Shape Up: Stop Running in Circles and Ship Work that Matters, available free online at basecamp.com/shapeup.

Core concepts

Shape Up organizes work into three phases — shaping, betting, and building — and structures time into six-week cycles followed by two-week cool-down periods.

item.title: Structured-work paradigms: a cross-disciplinary survey
item.description:
This text surveys structured-work methodologies across three disciplines — business/management, government/military, and scientific/academic research — to identify patterns that inform the design of agent workflows in the agential semioverse repository.

The survey was conducted via web research in March 2026. Each domain was researched independently to avoid cross-contamination of framing. Citations are provided per finding.

Cross-cutting patterns

Five patterns recur across all three domains. These are the findings most relevant to the emsemioverse’s workflow design.
